name: kachumber
instructions:
CHOP EVERYTHING FRESH: dice tomatoes, cucumbers, and red onions small (small dice — not minced, not chunky). Julienne the carrots and daikon (matchstick cut — see notes). Finely mince a green chili (serrano or jalapeño). Roughly chop a generous handful of fresh cilantro.

SEASON: combine in a bowl with a squeeze of lime (canonical) or lemon, ground cumin, garam masala, a small pinch of brown sugar (see notes), salt (only if adding chickpeas or rice — see notes), and freshly cracked black pepper.

OPTIONAL PROTEIN / CARB ADDITIONS: fold in cooked chickpeas (as protein) and/or cooked rice (as carb). Both should be added in LIMITED quantity relative to the fresh vegetables — kachumber's identity is the fresh dice, not the legume or grain.

LET IT SIT BRIEFLY (OPTIONAL): a few minutes of rest lets the onion bite mellow and the lime/lemon penetrate. Don't let it sit too long or the cucumbers weep and the salad goes watery.
notes:
WHAT THIS IS — KACHUMBER, NOT INVERTED CHANA MASALA: this recipe used to be 'inverted chana masala' (a pilaf experiment). On further inspection, what I'd accidentally arrived at — once stripped of the rice-fry technique and the heavy spice base — was kachumber, the classic indian fresh-chopped vegetable salad. Rewriting it under its actual name.

EVERYTHING FRESH: the entire identity of this dish is fresh-chopped raw vegetables. No cooking the tomatoes, no sauteing the onions, no warming anything. Tomato should be ripe, cucumber crisp, onion sharp, cilantro vibrant. If any of those is past its prime, the dish is dead — there's nothing to hide behind here.

SALT IS LIMITED — AND ONLY NEEDED WITH CHICKPEAS OR RICE: salt is only necessary if you've added chickpeas or rice as a protein/carb. Without those additions, the raw vegetables don't need salt at all — the lime, cumin, cilantro, and onion carry it. Even with chickpeas or rice in the mix, keep the salt limited; both the protein and the carb should themselves be limited quantities relative to the fresh vegetables, so the salt requirement stays modest.

LIME IS CANONICAL — LEMON IS FINE, ESPECIALLY WITH CHICKPEAS: traditional kachumber uses lime. Lemon works fine too, and is arguably better when chickpeas are in the mix as a protein — lemon's slightly rounder acidity pairs more cleanly with cooked chickpeas than lime's sharper edge. Either is acceptable; don't agonize over the choice.

GARAM MASALA — NOT CANONICAL BUT I LIKE IT A LOT HERE: traditional kachumber sticks to cumin (and sometimes chaat masala). Garam masala isn't part of the standard recipe, but I like what it does — the warm-spice depth bridges the raw-vegetable brightness with the cumin without crossing into curry territory. Optional; canonical-traditionalists will skip it.

LIMITED PROTEIN/CARB OR YOU LOSE THE DISH: chickpeas and rice are good additions but in restraint. The moment they outweigh the fresh vegetables the dish reads as a chickpea-and-rice salad with vegetables in it, rather than kachumber with chickpeas. Aim for the fresh-chopped vegetables (tomato + cucumber + onion + cilantro) to be the visual and textural majority on the fork.

NO PRE-MIXED DRESSING: kachumber isn't a dressed salad — the lime/lemon goes directly on the vegetables, the spices sprinkle on, no oil emulsion. This is one of the reasons everything has to be fresh; there's no dressing carrying the flavor.

JULIENNE THE CARROTS AND DAIKON — DON'T DICE: carrots and daikon get a julienne (matchstick) cut, not the small dice the tomatoes/cucumbers/onions get. The matchstick shape contributes a different texture register on the fork — long crisp threads against the small juicy cubes — and that contrast is part of what makes the salad read interesting. Diced carrot would disappear into the rest; matchstick carrot and daikon stand out.

DAIKON OVER REGULAR RADISH: daikon (the large mild japanese-style radish) is the right pick over small european radishes here. Regular radishes are too sharp/peppery and overwhelm the cumin and brown sugar; daikon is milder, juicier, and more textural — gives you the crunch without taking the dish over. If daikon isn't available, regular radishes can sub in but go light.

A PINCH OF BROWN SUGAR — BALANCES THE LIME AND CUMIN: a small pinch of brown sugar (not white) rounds out the lime's sharp acid and balances the cumin's earthiness. Brown sugar specifically — its molasses depth pairs with the cumin in a way white sugar doesn't. Closest indian-traditional analog would be jaggery; brown sugar is the western-pantry stand-in. Keep it small — kachumber isn't a sweet salad.

GREEN CHILI (SERRANO OR JALAPEÑO) IS CANONICAL: a finely-minced fresh green chili is part of traditional kachumber — the bright vegetal heat against the cool tomato/cucumber base is what makes the salad pop on the palate. Serrano is sharper and hotter; jalapeño is milder and more vegetal. Either works, pick based on what you have. Mince fine so the heat distributes rather than landing in single bites. Deseed if you want to dial the heat down further.
